citation.authors: Kouta Suzuki, Miaohui Shi, Vimolmongkolporn Vitvasin, Yukiko Iwasaki, Hiroyasu Iwata
citation.title: The Role of Automation and User Experience in Multi Robot Systems

extraction_metadata.notes: Not a swarm system — a single-operator 'multi-presence' setup with three independently-operated robotic manipulator arms (extensions of a 'detachable body' concept), not a coordinated multi-robot swarm. Included per the batch request; flagged here given this project's swarm focus. Exact participant count was not stated in the article text as extracted.

measured_constructs: Other / Not Listed (custom Multi-Presence questionnaire measuring presence, engagement, sense of achievement, and satisfaction)

study_type.justification: A controlled experiment with real participants operating physical robotic arms under three automation levels, using a dual-task design (grasping plus the PASAT attention test) with NASA-TLX, a custom multi-presence questionnaire, and objective grasp-score/PASAT-score measures, is the article's central validating evidence, making User Study Primary. The article also presents a detailed, novel 'Multi-Presence System' architecture (hardware, middleware, three-tier automation-level control scheme, AI perception pipeline) as a comparably-weighted contribution, warranting System/Architecture Paper as Secondary.

summary.task: Participants operated a lightweight robotic arm (MyCobot, VR-HMD-mounted camera head, gamepad control) to perform a grasping task on household objects (a sponge, a seasoning container, a toy truck) under three levels of automation (Level 1: fully manual Cartesian teleoperation with gripper toggle; Level 2: manual positioning with a one-button 'grasp-move-release' compound action; Level 3: the user only selects the target object via head-orientation ('Face Vector') and the system autonomously detects, estimates the pose of, and grasps it). While grasping, participants simultaneously performed the Paced Auditory Serial Addition Task (PASAT), a divided-attention/working-memory test, and experienced randomly injected robot failures (a stuck arm at Levels 1-2; a failed grasp at Level 3) to probe trust and failure tolerance.
summary.control: Levels 1 and 2 used a Logitech F710 gamepad: the left stick/D-pad controlled wrist and end-effector z-axis motion, the right stick controlled the arm's forward/backward/left/right movement, a green button reset the gripper, and a red button either toggled the gripper (Level 1) or triggered a scripted close-move-release sequence (Level 2). At Level 3, the user's only control action was selecting an object by orienting their head (visualized via a center-screen reticle in the VR HMD) and pressing the red button to confirm; the system then autonomously performed object detection (YOLOv8), 6D pose estimation (PVNet), coordinate transformation (MoveIt!), and grasping.
summary.participants: Number of participants and demographics are not explicitly stated in the article text as reviewed; the experiment used a within-subject design in which each participant completed practice and formal trials at all three automation levels, each paired with the PASAT.
summary.results: Grasp-task performance (objects successfully grasped) improved significantly as automation level increased. PASAT (secondary attention-task) scores were best at Level 3 (robot handled most of the task, freeing attention for PASAT) and worst at Level 2, plausibly because Level 2 still required active monitoring of arm movement on top of increased apparent complexity. NASA-TLX results showed no significant mental-demand difference between Levels 1 and 2, but Level 3 showed significantly lower mental demand than Level 1; temporal demand increased (did not improve) with automation level; required effort decreased significantly with higher automation; and overall system evaluation improved with automation level. The multi-presence questionnaire showed presence/engagement were largely unaffected by automation level, while sense of achievement, satisfaction, trust, and tolerance for system failures all increased with automation level (with sense-of-achievement gains diminishing at the highest level).
summary.issues: Increased automation improved efficiency and reduced effort but did not consistently reduce mental or temporal demand — Level 2 in particular showed elevated temporal demand and no mental-demand improvement over fully manual Level 1, since users still had to monitor and mentally track the arm's automated sub-movements. The measured repeatable positioning accuracy of the lightweight MyCobot arm (about 1 cm) was substantially worse than the manufacturer's claimed 0.5 mm, attributed to structural deformation from the added gripper weight, and is noted as detrimental to experimental precision. A subset of participants showed declining rather than improving PASAT scores across the session despite rising automation levels, attributed to cumulative mental fatigue across repeated trials that automation could not fully offset (a threat to interpreting the automation-benefit trend uniformly). Level 3's head-vector/Face-Vector selection interface, while eventually intuitive, required a substantially longer learning period than the near-zero learning curve observed transitioning between Levels 1 and 2.
summary.recommendations: Tailor automation level to context rather than assuming 'more automation is always better': use moderate automation for high-efficiency/high-freedom work settings, high automation to reduce cognitive load in high-mental-engagement contexts (e.g., education), and low automation where complex scenarios demand detailed manual interaction. Develop finer-grained, more comprehensive automation-level standards for robotic manipulation (analogous to autonomous-driving levels) to accommodate task diversity and environmental complexity beyond simple grasping. Explore richer input modalities for higher automation levels, such as voice commands and multimodal large-language-model-based interpretation (e.g., ChatGPT/Gemini-style command disambiguation and sub-task decomposition), to reduce the learning cost associated with novel selection interfaces like Face Vector. Balance weight against positioning accuracy deliberately when designing wearable/detachable robotic arms, since the tradeoff differs depending on whether the arm is worn (favor light weight) or operating detached/closed-loop (favor accuracy).
summary.autonomous_capabilities: At automation Level 3, the system autonomously performs the full grasp pipeline once the user selects a target: YOLOv8-based object detection identifies candidate objects and their bounding boxes; the appropriate PVNet 6D pose-estimation model (selected based on the chosen object's category) computes the object's pose from RGB images; camera and world coordinates are computed via MoveIt!; and the robotic arm autonomously executes the full pick sequence. A simplified predefined-position variant of Level 3 (used in the multi-presence control scheme) instead uses only YOLOv8 recognition plus preset item positions, avoiding pose estimation entirely to reduce computational load and improve stability. An event-driven task-response architecture with per-object event queues allows the system to route and execute commands for multiple independently-controlled robotic arms without cross-object interference.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: The interactive objects (sponge, seasoning container, toy truck) are multiple, static, and visible/known to the user and system from the start of each trial, not discovered via search — the same known-fixed-object gap flagged in several other corpus articles.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: The task is to grasp a known object and move/place it at a goal location — the same object-transport/delivery relation flagged as a gap in patel_2022_multi_human_multi_robot_remote_interaction and paul_2023_multimodal_hri_framework, not covered by Search, Avoidance, Engagement, Pursuit, or Defend/Block.

team_size.justification: The 'multi-presence' concept explicitly frames a single operator managing multiple independently-located robotic arms.

team_composition.justification: The robotic arms are identical MyCobot platforms with the same gripper/camera hardware; differences in automation level are a control-state parameter rather than a difference in physical capability or embodiment.
A single operator is described per session; no multi-human team is present.

interaction_model.justification: The multi-presence system's core premise is a single operator managing multiple robotic arms at different locations.

interaction_directness.justification: Gamepad commands and head-vector object selections are explicitly addressed to a specific selected arm/object.

communication_proximity.justification: The robots being operated were located on the opposite side of the room from the seated, HMD-wearing participant, mediated entirely through the VR/camera/gamepad system.

coordination_architecture.justification: The robotic arms are independently-controlled tools with no inter-robot coordination described; each responds only to its own event queue.

computational_characteristics.justification: Automation Levels 1 and 2 use direct teleoperation and a fixed scripted action sequence (close gripper, move above endpoint, release) rather than a computed search or learned model.
Automation Level 3 autonomously computes the grasp target using trained deep-learning models — YOLOv8 for object detection and PVNet for 6D pose estimation.

human_involvement_level.justification: The article's own classification (following Beer et al., 2014) places Level 1 as Human-In-Loop: the arm's motion requires continuous manual control and does not proceed without it.

The article's own classification places Levels 2 and 3 as (lower and higher, respectively) Human-On-Loop; the codebook's coarser three-level scale does not distinguish this 'lower vs. higher' HOnL nuance the authors themselves draw.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: Both Levels 1 and 2 require continuous joystick input mapping directly to the arm's Cartesian motion; per the 'minimum wins' rule, this continuous positioning input determines the state's code even though Level 2 also includes a discrete compound-action trigger.
At Level 3 the user gives a single discrete input (select and confirm a target object) and the system autonomously executes the entire remaining grasp sequence.

operational_domain.justification: The robotic arms are stationary/tabletop ground-based manipulators.

flags.note: Objects are known/visible from task start, not discovered via search.
flags.proposed_code: See bashyal_2008_radiation_source_hsi and related flags for 'Known Fixed Task Object.'

flags.note: Grasp-and-relocate is not covered by any existing behavioral-relation code.
The codebook's three-level HInL/HOnL/HOffL scale cannot represent the article's own finer 'lower HOL vs. higher HOL' distinction between automation Levels 2 and 3.
